overview: Gruul Ponza is a land destruction and ramp deck that aims to disrupt opponents' mana bases while accelerating its own mana to cast large creatures. It thrives against decks reliant on vulnerable lands, though it faces challenges against indestructible lands and mana-generating strategies. The deck typically follows an aggressive game plan, racing opponents or applying pressure with big threats after establishing board control.
win_conditions: Cast large creatures to attack and end the game
Race opponents by deploying threats quickly
Apply pressure with big creatures post-land destruction

keep_criteria: Hands with early ramp like Arbor Elf
Hands with playable creatures for an aggressive start
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing basics or duals that can untap), at least one 1-2 mana threat, and a land destruction spell.
Keep Utopia Sprawl only if paired with a 2-drop threat or ramp.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking early ramp or land destruction
Hands with only high-cost creatures without mana acceleration
Hands that will take too long to develop the game
Mulligan pure land, pure removal, or hands missing basic land sources needed for turn 1-2 plays.

examples.reason: Matches the perfect stance of casting Arbor Elf on turn 1, then ramping and destroying lands immediately.

early_game: Play Wild Growth or Utopia Sprawl to ramp
Turn 1: Play a basic Forest or dual land, cast a 1-mana threat or discard spell.
Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a 2-mana threat or Utopia Sprawl if you have a target.
Prioritize establishing board presence while simultaneously disrupting opponent’s mana with Acid-Moss/Thermokarst on their non-basics or key utility lands.
Against ramp, prioritize land destruction early to slow acceleration.
mid_game: Continue ramping to cast large creatures
Race opponents if they have indestructible lands
Optimize mana for casting threats and land destruction in the same turn.
Preserve hand disruption for key turns.
Don't overcommit to Utopia Sprawl without a reliable target
use it to close games or stabilize.
Sequence land destruction to break the most impactful lands first.
Commit threats when you've established mana denial or when the opponent is vulnerable to Utopia Sprawl.
late_game: Deploy big creatures to attack and end the game
Use sideboard options like Weather the Storm or Deglamer/Natural Obsolescence to handle specific matchups
Utopia Sprawl on a resilient threat combined with efficient creatures is the primary win condition.
Once mana is disrupted and board control is established, swing with boosted threats to pressure for lethal.
Go all-in when you've broken key mana, they're low on removal, or you have a clear path to lethal with Utopia Sprawl + threats.
Also commit when they're on the draw and you can pressure them before they stabilize.

key_cards.name: Arbor Elf
key_cards.notes: Ideal turn 1 play to accelerate mana for land destruction and creatures.

key_cards.name: Wild Growth
key_cards.notes: Follows Arbor Elf to quickly ramp into threats.

key_cards.name: Utopia Sprawl
key_cards.notes: Alternative ramp card to quickly develop the board.

key_cards.name: Deglamer
key_cards.notes: Shuffles indestructible lands back into the library.

key_cards.name: Natural Obsolescence
key_cards.notes: Returns indestructible lands to the deck, crucial post-side.

key_cards.name: Thermokarst
key_cards.notes: Key land destruction spell; speed of casting it is vital in the mirror.

key_cards.name: Weather the Storm
key_cards.notes: Sideboard card that counters burn strategies and buys time.

sequencing_tips: Prioritize casting Arbor Elf on turn 1 to enable early ramp
Follow ramp with land destruction to disrupt opponents immediately
Adjust aggression based on opponent's land types, racing if they run indestructible lands
Mulligan aggressively if the hand lacks early ramp or playable creatures
Common mistakes include overusing land destruction on non-impactful lands, holding Utopia Sprawl too long without a target, and mismanaging mana to cast threats and disruption in the same turn.
Play the long game, disrupt their plan, and close out with Utopia Sprawl + threats.
Ponza thrives in mid-to-late game resource wars and wins by out-resourcing opponents rather than racing.

matchups.advice: Race in game 1 by casting creatures quickly. Post-side, use Deglamer and Natural Obsolescence to shuffle indestructible lands back into their library.
Delay their game with land destruction and pressure them with big creatures. Matchup improves significantly post-side.
Likely the worst matchup. Side in area removal to mitigate their board, but expect to lose due to their mana generation and pseudo-counterspell effects.
Game can swing either way. Use sideboard options to give land destruction and aggro lines. Weather the Storm is crucial to survive burn damage.
Mirror match favors the player who casts Thermokarst first. Sideboard minimally or zero, but ensure you have faster land destruction.

Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter or counter them with discard. Sequence land destruction to break their mana so they can't cast counters later. Utopia Sprawl is cheap enough to cast in response to their mana development.
Prioritize early land destruction to slow acceleration. Use Utopia Sprawl to stabilize against early threats. Trade efficiently and don't let them get ahead on board.
Play early threats to race, use Utopia Sprawl to block and counter-attack, and hold land destruction for key utility lands. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and stabilize before going for the win.
Pressure with efficient threats, use land destruction to break mana and force basics, and use Utopia Sprawl to swing for lethal. Don't let them stabilize; keep pressure on and disrupt as needed.

Win the resource war by playing more efficiently, using land destruction to break their mana, and leveraging Utopia Sprawl to close out games. Prioritize hand disruption and efficient threats to out-value them.

common_threats: Watch opponent's land drops (basics vs non-basics), graveyard activity, and mana development.
Key tells: Holding mana on turn 3-4 often signals combo/control.
Playing multiple non-basics early suggests vulnerability to land destruction.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: it is a land destruction deck, but we only use 8 in the list: The idea of the deck is not only to give LD, but also to ramp up a lot to be able to cast the creatures that will win the game.

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: ramp up a lot to be able to cast the creatures that will win the game

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: The perfect stance is to cast Arbor Elf on turn 1, then cast Wild Growth or Utopia Sprawl and already destroy some land.

common_threats_evidence.evidence_span: Quirion Ranger that returns the Forests to their hand, serving as a pseudo-counterspell.
